explanation.text: The petals are described as feathery and yellow, orange, red, purple, and blue. Comparing them with fleecy rainbows therefore suggests both a fuzzy texture and many colors.
Because the hot-pink welt appears on Alex's arm after a biting insect attacks him, it is the raised bump left on his skin by the bite.

Professor Raglin begins with good news that the plant is not toxic, but his smile disappears before he lists its useless, unpleasant qualities and says it may be destroyed. The change therefore signals disappointing news.
Wendy has just learned that the flowers may be smelly and useless, yet she immediately finds one favorable fact: they are harmless. This response shows that she looks for a situation's positive side.

New Harmony has a severe infestation of biting “naggers,” and the insects unexpectedly avoid Woolly-Puffs. Because the colony needs relief from those pests, the flowers are kept as natural bug chasers.
Professor Raglin says the flowers smell like moldy lemons, and Wendy agrees that their odor is different. Thus, “smell funny” refers to an unusual scent, not something humorous.

Although the council has decided to remove the flowers, Wendy attends its meeting, presents her idea, and asks the members to watch the insects. Her courage to speak up therefore saves the useful plants.
Mrs. Majeska enjoys the brisk wind, dancing leaves, glorious weather, and anticipation of a favorite meal. Wanting to dance along therefore expresses how happy she feels while enjoying the day.

Mrs. Majeska feels sorry for the owner and asks what good one glove could be by itself. This early concern foreshadows her later decision to reunite the matching gloves so they remain useful together.
The children treat the second glove as worthless debris and kick it through the dust. Mrs. Majeska recognizes it as the missing mate and understands that joining the pair restores its value.

Mrs. Majeska retrieves, cleans, and carries the second glove back to its mate, then feels satisfied seeing the pair together. Her contentment demonstrates the central importance she places on restoring what belongs together.
When a bicyclist and two women approach, Mrs. Majeska pretends to tie her shoe so they will not see her reuniting the gloves. This behavior shows concern that strangers may think she looks foolish.
Mrs. Majeska wishes she had taken the gloves, decides to return for them, and finds them gone. Her second thoughts and failed attempt to retrieve them show that she is regretful.
Readers hear Mrs. Majeska's mystified thoughts as she imagines several possible reasons the gloves disappeared. Her point of view reveals her surprise that anyone else would take them.

The article traces how Roebling created the first American iron cable, used it to modernize suspension bridges, and designed increasingly ambitious structures. These achievements therefore establish him as an inspired engineer.
Once tests proved the cables thinner, stronger, and longer-lasting than rope, delighted business owners began buying them. “Snatching up” therefore suggests the cables sold quickly.
Paragraph 4 explains that modern suspension bridges use thick metal cables attached to strong towers to hold the roadway. The Brooklyn Bridge photograph clearly shows those cables and towers.

During the Civil War, Washington builds temporary suspension bridges using his father's ideas and later becomes his chief engineer. This demonstrates that he applied the bridge knowledge John had taught him.

Paragraph 7 contrasts New Yorkers who call the bridge wasteful with Brooklyn residents who favor it. Paragraph 10 therefore adds safety worries and the experts' approval, introducing more opinions about the project.
Ice on the East River stopped ferry service for days, showing how vulnerable travel between the cities was. That disruption persuaded Brooklyn's mayor and other leaders that a bridge was necessary.

Roebling's creation of the first iron wire cable in the United States made stronger modern suspension bridges possible and shaped his later work. This major innovation therefore belongs in a summary more than biographical details.
